hotspots.title: Database connection opened inside loop
hotspots.description: DriverManager.getConnection() is called on each loop iteration. Connection establishment is expensive in CPU, memory, and I/O. This also bypasses connection pooling.
hotspots.energy_impact: Creates O(n) TCP handshakes and authentication round-trips instead of 1.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Open the connection once before the loop and reuse it.

hotspots.title: N+1 query problem — individual queries per order ID
hotspots.description: Executes one SELECT per order ID instead of a single batched query. Each query incurs parsing, planning, execution, and network round-trip overhead.
hotspots.energy_impact: O(n) queries where 1 suffices; network I/O and DB CPU grow linearly with order count.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Use SELECT * FROM orders WHERE id IN (?, ?, ...) with a single PreparedStatement or a batch.

hotspots.title: String concatenation in loop building SQL
hotspots.description: sql += ... inside the loop creates a new String object at each iteration (O(n²) copying). Strings are immutable in Java, so each append copies the entire accumulated string.
hotspots.energy_impact: O(n²) time and memory for string building; increased GC pressure.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Use StringBuilder to build the query incrementally, or better, use a PreparedStatement with batch parameters.

hotspots.title: Statement created inside loop instead of using PreparedStatement
hotspots.description: A new Statement is created per iteration, and raw SQL concatenation prevents query plan caching. PreparedStatement would allow the DB to cache the execution plan.
hotspots.energy_impact: Database re-parses and re-optimizes the same query shape on every iteration.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Create a single PreparedStatement outside the loop and bind parameters.
hotspots.estimated_savings: ~50% reduction in query planning CPU on the DB side

hotspots.title: SQL injection vulnerability (also prevents plan caching)
hotspots.description: String concatenation of order IDs directly into SQL is a security vulnerability and prevents the database from reusing cached query plans.
hotspots.energy_impact: Forces a new hard parse on every execution; no plan reuse possible.
hotspots.estimated_savings: Enables query plan caching across calls

hotspots.title: String concatenation in result processing loop
hotspots.description: rs.getString(...) + ',' + rs.getString(...) creates intermediate String objects. Inside a while loop over many rows, this compounds memory and GC overhead.
hotspots.energy_impact: Each row produces throwaway String objects; increased GC frequency.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Use StringBuilder for building each result string.

hotspots.title: File read byte-by-byte without buffering
hotspots.description: FileInputStream.read() reads one byte at a time, causing a system call per byte. This is extremely I/O inefficient — several orders of magnitude slower than buffered reads.
hotspots.energy_impact: O(n) system calls instead of O(n/bufferSize); CPU burns on kernel transitions.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Wrap in BufferedInputStream or use BufferedReader.readLine() for text files.

hotspots.title: String concatenation building file content byte-by-byte
hotspots.description: content += (char) b inside the loop creates a new String per byte read. For a config file of even modest size, this creates massive allocation pressure.
hotspots.energy_impact: O(n²) time and memory for string building; heavy GC churn.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Use StringBuilder or read the entire file with Files.readString() / readAllBytes().
hotspots.estimated_savings: Eliminates O(n²) copying from byte-level concatenation

hotspots.title: FileInputStream not closed in finally block / try-with-resources
hotspots.description: If an exception occurs during read, fis.close() is never called, leaking the file descriptor. While not directly an energy issue, leaked descriptors degrade system performance over time.
hotspots.energy_impact: Potential resource leak leading to degraded system performance.

hotspots.title: O(n²) nested loop for duplicate detection
hotspots.description: Two nested loops over the entire list yield O(n²) comparisons. For a list of 10,000 elements, this is 100M comparisons instead of ~10K with a HashSet approach.
hotspots.energy_impact: CPU time grows quadratically with input size; wasteful comparisons dominate runtime.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Use a HashSet to track seen elements in a single O(n) pass.
hotspots.estimated_savings: O(n) instead of O(n²) — up to 99.99% fewer comparisons for large n

hotspots.title: Duplicate elements added multiple times (algorithmic bug)
hotspots.description: For a value appearing k times, it gets added to dupes k*(k-1) times instead of once. E.g., [1,1,1] produces [1,1,1,1,1,1]. This wastes memory and processing downstream.
hotspots.energy_impact: Unnecessary memory allocation and processing for spurious duplicate entries.
hotspots.recommended_fix: Track duplicates in a Set and add only once per duplicate value.
hotspots.estimated_savings: Eliminates redundant entries; reduces memory and processing

hotspots.title: Diamond operator not used — redundant type parameter
hotspots.description: new ArrayList<String>() repeats the type parameter. Since Java 7, the diamond operator <> infers the type. This is a minor compilation detail but reflects outdated code patterns.
hotspots.energy_impact: Negligible at runtime; purely a code style issue.

prioritized_issues.title: Database connection inside loop
prioritized_issues.rationale: Most expensive single operation — network + auth per iteration. Fix enables all subsequent DB optimizations.

prioritized_issues.title: O(n²) duplicate detection
prioritized_issues.rationale: Algorithmic complexity class change. Quadratic runtime dominates at scale.

prioritized_issues.title: N+1 query problem
prioritized_issues.rationale: Eliminating individual queries per ID is the single biggest DB-side win.

prioritized_issues.title: Byte-by-byte file read
prioritized_issues.rationale: System call per byte is catastrophic for I/O-bound operations.

prioritized_issues.title: Statement per iteration / no PreparedStatement
prioritized_issues.rationale: Enables query plan caching and reduces DB CPU.

prioritized_issues.title: String concatenation in SQL loop
prioritized_issues.rationale: O(n²) copying; worsens with larger order lists.

prioritized_issues.title: String concatenation in file read loop
Duplicate bug in findDuplicates
prioritized_issues.rationale: Algorithmic correctness issue causing wasted memory.

prioritized_issues.title: SQL injection / no plan caching
prioritized_issues.rationale: Security + performance; prevented by fixing H04.

prioritized_issues.title: String concatenation in result processing
prioritized_issues.rationale: Compounds with row count; lower priority than loop-level issues.

prioritized_issues.title: Resource leak in readConfig
prioritized_issues.rationale: Important for reliability; less direct energy impact.

prioritized_issues.title: Redundant type parameter
prioritized_issues.rationale: Code style only; negligible runtime impact.

immediate_actions: Refactor processOrders: move Connection outside loop, use single PreparedStatement with batched IDs or IN clause, use StringBuilder for SQL construction.
Refactor findDuplicates: replace O(n²) nested loops with a single O(n) pass using HashSet. Fix the duplicate-multiple-adds bug.
short_term: Wrap FileInputStream in BufferedInputStream or use BufferedReader for readConfig. Use StringBuilder for content accumulation.
Add try-with-resources to all I/O and JDBC operations for deterministic cleanup.
Prefer parameterized queries (PreparedStatement) for all SQL operations.
long_term: Adopt connection pooling (e.g., HikariCP) instead of DriverManager.getConnection().
Introduce a static code analysis tool (SpotBugs, PMD) to catch energy-inefficient patterns early.
Consider frameworks like Spring JDBC Template or JOOQ that handle connection/statement management efficiently.

summary: The file scores 18/100 on energy efficiency. All three methods exhibit severe inefficiencies: processOrders has a classic N+1 query anti-pattern with per-iteration connection and statement creation; readConfig reads a file byte-by-byte with unbuffered I/O; findDuplicates uses an O(n²) brute-force algorithm. The most impactful fixes are: (1) hoist DB connection and use batch queries, (2) replace nested loops with HashSet for O(n) duplicate detection, and (3) buffer file I/O. Estimated combined energy reduction: 70–90% for typical workloads.
